title: Satellites
subtitle: Natural and artificial objects in orbit

steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: A geostationary satellite…
options: Orbits Earth every 90 minutes at low altitude
Stays above the same point on Earth’s surface with a 24-hour period
Orbits the Sun rather than the Earth
Moves from pole to pole, scanning the entire surface every day
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! A geostationary satellite orbits at ~35,786 km altitude with a period of exactly 24 hours, matching Earth’s rotation. This means it appears stationary above one fixed point — ideal for communications.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! Geostationary satellites have a 24-hour orbital period and stay above the same point on Earth’s equator — making them ideal for TV and communications.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA Physics specification 4.8.1 — Satellites

steps.title: Satellites
steps.bodyHTML: Natural satellites (such as the Moon) and artificial satellites orbit planets due to gravity. Geostationary satellites orbit at ~35,786 km altitude with a period of 24 hours, staying fixed above one point on the equator — used for communications and TV. Low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites orbit at a few hundred km altitude with much shorter periods (~90 minutes), moving quickly across the sky; they are used for remote sensing, weather monitoring, and the International Space Station.
commonErrors: Thinking geostationary satellites are stationary in space — they are moving, but at the same angular speed as Earth’s rotation
Confusing the altitude of geostationary (very high, 35,786 km) with LEO (a few hundred km)
Forgetting that a lower orbit means a faster speed AND a shorter period

aqa.questionText: Compare geostationary satellites and low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites in terms of their altitude, orbital period and typical uses.

steps.label: Compare altitude
steps.content: Geostationary satellites orbit at ~35,786 km. LEO satellites orbit at a few hundred to a few thousand km.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — geostationary much higher altitude than LEO

steps.label: Compare orbital period
steps.content: Geostationary satellites have a period of 24 hours, matching Earth’s rotation. LEO satellites have periods of around 90 minutes.

steps.label: Geostationary uses
steps.content: Geostationary satellites are used for communications, satellite TV and GPS (because they remain above a fixed point).

steps.label: LEO uses
steps.content: LEO satellites are used for Earth observation, weather forecasting, remote sensing and the International Space Station.

aqa.finalAnswer: Geostationary: ~35,786 km, 24-hour period, communications/TV. LEO: ~200–2000 km, ~90-minute period, remote sensing/weather.

aqa.questionText: Why is a geostationary orbit preferred for satellite television broadcasts?

options.label: A
options.text: Geostationary satellites travel faster, so they can transmit more data

options.label: B
options.text: The satellite remains above the same point on Earth, so a dish antenna can stay fixed

options.label: C
options.text: Geostationary satellites are closer to Earth, so the signal is stronger

options.label: D
options.text: Geostationary orbits avoid the Van Allen radiation belts

markSchemeSteps: A fixed position above the same point means a dish on the ground does not need to track or move – essential for reliable TV broadcast ✓
aqa.commonMistake: Option C is wrong — geostationary satellites are actually very far away (35,786 km), much further than LEO satellites.
